lens.included_activities: No qualifying recurring outsourced-service activity was identified inside the official 525120 definition; the code consists of funds, plans, or programs providing health- and welfare-related employee benefits exclusively for a sponsor's employees or members.
lens.excluded_activities: Sponsor- or member-only health and welfare funds, captive benefit arrangements, non-control financial vehicles, shells, portfolio managers classified in 523920, third-party claims administrators classified in 524292, insurance carriers, and other external service providers classified outside this code.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The classified entity is a benefit fund rather than a fee-earning operator serving external customers. It finances or provides medical, surgical, hospital, vacation, training, or other welfare benefits for the sponsor's employees or members; separable portfolio management and claims administration are supplied by entities in other industries.

a.rationale: There is no eligible outsourced-service firm population in the official code to support a wage-weighted share of exposed tasks. Health and welfare funds may perform or procure enrollment, eligibility, claims oversight, reporting, participant communication, accounting, and compliance work, but those activities cannot be converted into the lens's operator labor share, and the frozen labor-share input is unavailable.
a.change_evidence: Evidence that fee-earning external health-benefit service operators are substantively classified in 525120, followed by payroll-by-occupation and task-time data separating enrollment, eligibility, claims, participant service, reporting, accounting, compliance, clinical review, and already-automated work, would be required to estimate a.
caveats: The official classification places third-party claims administration in NAICS 524292 and fund portfolio management in 523920.
Form 5500 work demonstrates administrative activity but does not create an external customer-and-revenue model for the fund itself.
The frozen labor-share input is missing and was not replaced or re-estimated.

rho.rationale: Without an eligible operator population or defensible exposed labor quantity, there is no meaningful denominator for five-year implementation. Electronic reporting and structured eligibility and claims data suggest technical opportunities, but implementation by sponsor-controlled funds or separately classified administrators is not implementation by a screened 525120 operator.
rho.change_evidence: A verified population of fee-earning 525120 operators plus deployment data on enrollment, claims, document processing, participant service, compliance review, error rates, appeals, human overrides, staffing, and realized labor release would be required to estimate rho.
caveats: Mandatory electronic reporting is evidence of digital workflow, not AI deployment or realized labor savings.
Protected health information, benefit accuracy, appeals, fiduciary oversight, and regulatory compliance would constrain implementation even if a valid operator population existed.
No broad adoption proxy can repair the missing lens denominator.

e.rationale: The Census definition places legal health and welfare funds serving only a sponsor's employees or members in 525120 and explicitly places portfolio management in 523920 and third-party claims administration in 524292. Under the fixed lens, the defined entities are captive or non-control benefit vehicles rather than external recurring-service firms; a small high bound allows for miscoding or an unusual independently controlled structure without importing adjacent industries.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification audit using Census Business Register records, Form 5500 service-provider schedules, fund documents, customer contracts, external fee revenue, control rights, claims roles, and normalized EBITDA could identify any exceptional 525120 entities that actually serve external customers and materially change eligibility.
caveats: The estimate is a structural lens judgment from the industry definition, not a measured firm census.
Multiemployer welfare arrangements may cover employees of multiple employers yet remain benefit vehicles rather than fee-earning third-party operators.
The frozen LMM firm count is missing and was not replaced with plan counts, participant counts, filings, claims, or assets.

s5.rationale: A qualifying control-transfer probability cannot be defined because the eligible-firm denominator is absent. Sponsor changes, trustee changes, fund mergers, plan combinations, administrator replacements, and internal reorganizations are not necessarily acquisitions of transferable outsourced-service operators.
s5.change_evidence: A verified list of eligible fee-earning 525120 firms and five years of completed control transactions involving those exact operating companies, excluding plan mergers, sponsor reorganizations, administrator mandates, and benefit-vehicle terminations, would be required to estimate s5.
caveats: Fund or plan events are not interchangeable with qualifying control transfers of operating firms.
The frozen firm-count denominator is missing.
No public transaction series was found for the nonexistent screened population.

q.rationale: The classified fund does not have a commercial pricing contract under which an operator retains an implemented gross benefit after customer sharing or repricing. Savings would remain in the benefit arrangement or flow through contracts with administrators, insurers, and investment providers classified elsewhere, so q is undefined for the frozen lens.
q.change_evidence: Evidence of eligible 525120 operating companies with external fee contracts, billing units, claim or participant pricing, renewals, gainsharing, technology costs, and realized margins after automation would be required to estimate q.
caveats: Reduced fund expenses or paid claims are not commercial benefit retention by an outsourced operator.
Third-party-administration and portfolio-management economics belong to other NAICS industries.
No receipts denominator exists for the frozen dataset input.

d5.rationale: Health and welfare benefits, enrollment, claims, and reporting create continuing activity, but there is no external service basket supplied by eligible 525120 operators whose constant-price quantity can be compared over five years. Benefit payments, covered lives, claims volume, and outsourced administration are different quantities.
d5.change_evidence: A defined eligible operator population plus a constant-price series of external health-benefit service contracts, covered participants, administered claims, renewals, and fee revenue for entities actually classified in 525120 would be required to estimate d5.
caveats: Form 5500 reporting obligations demonstrate recurring plan administration but not lens-qualified market demand.
Claims or covered lives cannot substitute for constant-price operator service quantity without a valid operating-company population.
Adjacent third-party administrator demand belongs to NAICS 524292 rather than this code.

o.rationale: There is no lens-qualified year-five service quantity to divide between accountable operators, self-service, and software. Benefit-plan accountability and appeals may persist, but the accountable entity is a fund, sponsor, insurer, or separately classified administrator rather than an eligible operator of this code.
o.change_evidence: A verified 525120 external-service basket and buyer-level evidence on software substitution, self-service, retained fiduciary and claims accountability, vendor replacement, appeals, and contract cancellations would be required to estimate o.
caveats: Persistent benefit-plan accountability does not establish an eligible outsourced operator within 525120.
Software may change enrollment or claims administration without changing the classification mismatch.
The denominator is absent rather than economically zero.

narrative.executive_view: NAICS 525120 is a category of sponsor- or member-only health and welfare benefit vehicles, not fee-earning outsourced-service firms. Its enrollment, claims oversight, reporting, and communications are digitally intensive, but portfolio managers and third-party claims administrators are explicitly classified elsewhere.
narrative.ai_impact: AI could assist document intake, eligibility checks, reporting, participant communications, claim triage, reconciliation, and compliance review in the surrounding benefit ecosystem. Those opportunities accrue to sponsors, funds, insurers, or separately classified administrators and cannot be translated into this code's frozen operator lens.
narrative.value_capture: Any savings inside a health and welfare fund are benefit expenses avoided or fund resources preserved, not commercial benefit retained under an external customer pricing model. Administrator, carrier, and investment-provider pricing belongs to adjacent industries.
narrative.firm_availability: Health and welfare funds can merge, terminate, change trustees, or replace administrators, but those events are not qualifying acquisitions of recurring outsourced-service operators. The missing frozen firm count remains unreplaced.
narrative.demand_durability: Health and welfare benefits and reporting create persistent operational needs, but the relevant service demand is provided by insurers, third-party administrators, advisers, and investment managers classified elsewhere. Fund obligations do not define a valid service basket for eligible 525120 firms.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central risk is category error: treating benefit payments, covered lives, sponsor-controlled vehicles, claims, administrator mandates, or fund events as operating-company revenue and transactions. Privacy, cybersecurity, claims accuracy, appeals, fiduciary duties, and reporting remain important but do not cure the lens mismatch.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring digital eligibility, claims, reporting, and participant-service workflows indicate automation potential in the surrounding benefits ecosystem.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code contains sponsor- or member-only benefit vehicles rather than external recurring-service firms, and both frozen dataset anchors are missing.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Sector 52 Definitions: Health and Welfare Funds
sources.supports: Defines 525120 as legal funds, plans, or programs providing health- and welfare-related benefits exclusively for a sponsor's employees or members and places portfolio management in 523920 and third-party claims administration in 524292.

sources.title: U.S. Department of Labor: Form 5500 Series
sources.supports: Describes the Form 5500 Series as the ERISA and Internal Revenue Code reporting and disclosure framework for employee benefit plans and requires electronic filing of covered annual reports and schedules.
